questions.question: Briefly explain the 'one-to-many' mapping characteristic of Third Generation Languages and its significance.
keyPoints: Refers to a single high-level statement compiling to multiple machine instructions.
This is a key differentiator from 2GLs which have a one-to-one mapping.
It enables greater programmer productivity and abstraction from hardware.
It is the reason 3GLs require a full compiler (not just an assembler).

questions.question: Trace the evolution of programming languages through the first four generations. For each generation, state its name, a key characteristic, and one example language. Finally, explain how this evolution has influenced the design and complexity of modern compilers.
keyPoints: 1GL: Machine Code - Binary execution, example: CPU instructions. Characteristic: No abstraction.
2GL: Assembly Language - Mnemonics, example: NASM. Characteristic: One-to-one mapping, requires an assembler.
3GL: High-Level Language - Abstraction, examples: C, FORTRAN. Characteristic: One-to-many mapping, requires a compiler.
4GL: Very High-Level Language - Declarative, example: SQL. Characteristic: Focus on 'what' not 'how'.
Compiler Influence: Higher abstraction requires more complex compilers with multiple phases (lexical, syntax, semantic analysis, optimization).
New paradigms (OOP, functional) introduce complex features (inheritance, closures) that the compiler's semantic analyzer and code generator must handle.
The trend continues with managed runtimes (e.g., JVM) adding another layer of abstraction for the compiler to target.
